title: Robust Measures of Earnings Surprises
abstract: Event studies of market efficiency measure earnings surprises using the consensus error (CE), given as actual earnings minus the average professional forecast. If a subset of forecasts can be biased, the ideal but difficult to estimate parameter-dependent alternative toCEis a nonlinear filter of individual errors that adjusts for bias. We show thatCEis a poor parameter-free approximation of this ideal measure. The fraction of misses on the same side (FOM), which discards the magnitude of misses, offers a far better approximation.FOMperforms particularly well againstCEin predicting the returns of U.S. stocks, where bias is potentially large.

article.introduction:
Economists historically measure the degreeto which the market is surprised by an earnings announcement using the consensus error, a simple parameter-free measure given as the difference between actual earnings and the consensus forecast, where the consensus is calculated as the mean of the available professional forecasts. The consensus error is widely used in financial markets. For instance, commentaries frequently refer to the extent to which earnings missed the consensus forecast in explaining significant stock price movements. The consensus error is also a commonly used component of event studies on the efficiency with which markets react to earnings news (see MacKinlay (1997), Lyon, Barber, and Tsai (1999), Kothari and Warner (2007)).
A canonical specification in such event studies regresses the cumulative abnormal return of a stock around the earnings announcement date (CAR) or subsequent to the announcement date (POSTCAR) on the consensus error (CE): the more positive is the consensus errorCE, the higher areCARandPOSTCAR(see, e.g., Bernard and Thomas (1990)). These regressions indicate that markets react to earnings news gradually and have become a linchpin in the debate on market efficiency (see Fama (1998)).
The widespread use of this measure is premised on the consensus forecast being an unbiased measure of the market's expectation of earnings. But it is well known that a subset of professional earnings forecasts can be biased. One important reason relates to analysts' conflicts of interest. A large literature shows that a number of firms and analysts engage in an earnings guidance game whereby analysts make optimistic forecasts at the start of the year and then walk their estimates down to a level the firm can beat by the end of the year (see, e.g., Richardson, Teoh, and Wysocki (2004)).1These biases in earnings forecasts tend to be stronger in the U.S. stock market, where firm issuance incentives are more likely to distort earnings forecasts, than in international markets (see, e.g., Chan, Karceski, and Lakonishok (2007)). Moreover, it may be optimal for analysts to strategically shade their forecasts, whether positively or negatively, away from their unbiased signal if the rewards to the forecasting tournament are sufficiently convex (see, e.g., Laster, Bennett, and Geoum (1999), Hong, Kubik, and Solomon (2000), Ottaviani and Sørensen (2006)). In short, some fraction of individual forecasts can be significantly biased depending on analysts' exposure to different incentives.
Since many investors—particularly institutions, which comprise a significant fraction of the market—attempt to adjust for these strategic forecast biases in forming their earnings expectations (see, e.g., Iskoz (2003), Malmendier and Shanthikumar (2007), Mikhail, Walther, and Willis (2007)), the end result is that the consensus forecast is no longer an unbiased measure of the market's expectation of earnings. In other words, by averaging biased analyst forecasts, the consensus forecast systematically diverges from the true expectation of the market. In the context of theCARandPOSTCARregressions, we ideally want an accurate and unbiased measure of the true market surprise as the explanatory variable on the right-hand side. IfCEas a proxy for the true market surprise has substantial measurement error, this would translate into poor explanatory power forCARorPOSTCARin these canonical regressions, and thereby leave room for a better measure of the true market surprise.
The challenge from the econometrician's point of view is how to construct this better measure, such that, like theCE, it has the advantage of being parameter-free but at the same time it takes as given the fact that the econometrician does not have the same information set as institutional investors. The usual robust statistics such as medians or winsorization cannot help as these statistics are meant to deal with outliers, not the systematic bias of forecasts.
To address this problem, we first articulate a tractable and empirically sensible model of earnings and forecasts where some fraction of individual forecasts can be biased. This bias has an aggregate component that can have a nonzero mean and variance. We then derive the ideal measure of the true earnings surprise, using the filter of individual forecast errors that is maximally correlated with the true surprise assuming that the parameters governing the individual forecasts are known. In general, this nonlinear filter of individual forecast errors accounts for biases and precision. We show that this nonlinear filter optimally downweights extreme misses of individual forecasts when there is potential for bias. Even if the bias has zero mean, the optimal filter will downweight extreme misses if there is ex-ante uncertainty or variance in the size of the aggregate bias. However, it is difficult to estimate the underlying parameters of individual forecasts to implement this filter in practice, as we show below. This is no doubt an important reason why the literature continues to useCE, which is parameter-free, in the face of extensive evidence of individual forecast biases.
We next develop a robustness criterion whereby the robustness of a parameter-free measure of earnings surprise, be itCEor some other measure, is evaluated by how well it approximates this ideal measure. We show that the efficiency ofCErelative to this full-information benchmark is close to zero when the potential bias is large, that is,CEis not a robust earnings surprise measure.
The fraction of misses is equivalent to taking the average of the signs (either −1, 0, or 1) of individual forecast errors. As a result, it discards information on the magnitude of misses much in the same way that the ideal filter would downweight large misses when potential bias is significant. We provide a theoretical lower bound on the efficiency ofFOMrelative to this full-information benchmark. The lower bound on the relative efficiency ofFOMcan be as high as 50% using plausible parameter values. Thus,FOMshares the advantage ofCEof being parameter-free but is less sensitive to biased forecasts and hence is potentially superior toCE. While we use earnings forecasts to frame our model and motivate our empirical analysis, the methodology and idea can be readily applied to other types of economic forecasts where bias is potentially important.
Taking magnitudes into account, as is the case using the traditional consensus error measure, when some forecasts are biased, sorting occurs on bias rather than the true market surprise. When we run the regression ofCARorPOSTCARonCE, whereCEis supposed to proxy for the true earnings surprise, measurement error arises and hence the coefficient onCEwill be downward biased. SinceFOMis more robust in that it tracks the market surprise better thanCE, we expect a regression ofCARorPOSTCARonFOMto have superior explanatory power relative toCEwhen potential bias is important.
Using the median forecast rather than the mean as the consensus does not help theCEmeasure since medians address outliers but not a significant fraction of the forecasts being biased. In practice, event studies are implemented using a transformation ofCEinto a cross-sectional decile score from 1 to 10, which we callRank(CE). TheRank(CE)measure addresses outliers and offers a better fit forCARandPOSTCARthanCE(see Hirshleifer, Lim, and Teoh (2009)). However, these rankings are a form of winsorization and thus deal with outliers. They do not remedy biases that significantly affectCEand the relative rankings of stocks that are considered to have positive or negative surprises.
UsingCEconstructed using the median of recent forecasts for the annual year-end FY1 earnings and the alternativeFOM, we test the predictions of our model.2Every firm has one observation per year over the 1983 to 2015 sample period. In a canonical regression ofCAR(measured using the three-day firm-size-adjusted return around the announcement date), a one-standard-deviation increase inRank(CE)increases theCARby 1.4%, a sizeable economic effect. ForPOSTCAR, the portfolio long positive earnings surprises and short negative earnings surprises yields a return of 68 bps over the subsequent six months (126 trading days to be exact) after the announcement date, or 1.4% annualized.
OurFOMvariable, however, performs better thanCEorRank(CE). A one-standard-deviation increase inFOMincreasesCARby 1.5%. The improvement overRank(CE)in terms ofR2is nearly 20%. When we run a horse race ofFOMandRank(CE), the coefficient onFOMis largely unchanged, whereas that onRank(CE)declines significantly. ForPOSTCAR, a portfolio longFOM=1stocks and shortFOM=−1stocks yields a six-month subsequent return of 2.5%, or 5% annualized. Again, in a multiple regression to explainPOSTCAR, ourFOMmeasure remains significant, whereasRank(CE)is insignificant.
Rather than using OLS, we reestimate our model using maximum likelihood so we can implement the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC) for nonnested model selection, that is, choose betweenRank(CE)andFOMto explainCARandPOSTCAR. The difference in BIC (384 forCARand 25 forPOSTCAR) far exceeds 10, which points to the overwhelming preference forFOMas the independent variable in a model to explainCARandPOSTCAR. When we also conduct nested likelihood ratio tests to compare a model ofCARandPOSTCARusingFOMversus using bothFOMandRank(CE), we cannot reject the hypothesis that the univariate model withFOMis sufficient for thePOSTCARregression.
In additional analyses, we conduct these BIC tests for international stocks, where potential bias is smaller than in U.S. stocks. Consistent with our model, we find that the incremental improvement ofFOMcompared toRank(CE)is smaller for international stocks. We also relate ourFOMmeasure to the walk-down effect in earnings forecasts and earnings persistence. Finally, we show that a parameter-dependent method that involves estimating individual analyst bias parameters based on their forecast histories and adjusting individual forecasts before calculatingCE(Rank(CE)) yields equally poor results as using the parameter-freeCE(Rank(CE)).
Our paper proceeds as follows. In SectionI, we present a model to evaluate the robustness of alternative parameter-free earnings surprise measures. In SectionII, we describe our data and how we construct our key variables of interest. We present our main empirical findings in SectionIII. SectionIVconcludes. An Appendix provides proofs and an Internet Appendix provides further discussions and extensions of our model to account for various aspects of the data.3
article.I. Modeling the Robustness of Earnings S:
In this section, we develop a model to evaluate the robustness of parameter-free measures of earnings surprises when analyst forecasts are potentially biased.
whereεi∼N(0,σF2)and is uncorrelated withεA. Each forecast is unbiased with probability ω0and is contaminated by an individual bias termbiwith probabilityω1=1−ω0.
We model the bias in the following manner. The individual bias termbifollowsN(B,σb2), whereBrepresents the aggregate bias level. We allow this aggregate bias levelBto vary across different sets of forecasts by modeling it asB∼N(μB,σB2). Note that while ω0and ω1are fixed and do not change withN, the realized number of biased forecasts can be different from its expectationω1N. Therefore, conditional on each set ofNforecasts, on average a fraction ω1of them are biased by a random magnitude.
According to this setup, the market is able to figure out which forecasts are biased and has access to information about the mean of earningsebeyond analyst forecasts. The variableεAis the unexpected shock to earnings, which the market cannot know. The biasbican be derived in a number of ways as we discussed above. As we discuss in detail in the Internet Appendix, many theories of forecast bias feature two types of forecasters, a group of unbiased forecasters and a group that is biased. While forecasters typically have incentives for accuracy, a subset of them may have other incentives that skew their forecasts, including career concerns (Scharfstein and Stein (1990)), a need to access management due to a lack of other sources of information (Lim (2001)), and a need to cater to the media or occasional users (Laster, Bennett, and Geoum (1999)).
Our empirical specification using a two-component mixture-normal for these two groups is also well motivated. We show in the Internet Appendix that analyst forecast errors do not follow a normal distribution using Shapiro and Wilk (1965) but rather are better described by a two-component mixture-normal using BIC tests. Theory points only to bias and precision being distinct, with the parameters governing the data-generating process likely to be related in complex ways depending on the situation and unknown to the econometrician. Theory does not yet offer guidance on the level of ω0. It may vary over time as well. This is all the more reason to develop a parameter-free robust estimator for market surprises, to which we now turn.
The optimal choicef∗(U)is highly nonlinear and in general cannot be analytically derived. It depends on many unknown parameters (ω0,μB,σB2,σb2,σF2, andσA2). Since these parameters are not known to the econometrician, in practice it is difficult to implement this optimal measure as we demonstrate in SectionIII.G.
In Figures1and2, we plotf∗=E[S|U],CE, andFOMin the simple case of one analyst (N=1) where analytical formulas allow for ease of comparison. These figures depict the shape of the optimal transformationf∗=E[S|U]for a few different configurations (differentσB's in Figure1and differentμB's in Figure2) as a function of the single forecast errorU. Recall thatw0is just the probability that the forecast is unbiased. It is highly nonlinear and asymmetric inUwhenμB≠0. When the aggregate biasBis positive (negative) on average,Uis more likely to be negatively (positively) biased, so the ideal measure discounts negative (positive) values ofUmore. Also notice how the optimal measure levels off asU's magnitude increases in both directions—because of the existence of biases, the benefit of ignoring the magnitude outweighs the loss of information. Our proposed measureFOMis motivated by this insight.
These relative efficiency calculations provide a metric to quantify the robustness ofFOMversusCE. Notice that these calculations depend onσBas opposed toμB. While the discussion above shows that both play a role in shaping the bias of the earnings surprise measures, we focus onσBfor a few reasons. First, the empirical evidence strongly points to a large degree of potential bias on both the positive and the negative side depending on incentives and forecast horizons. Second, from Lemma1,σBremains a large source of bias relative toμBeven whenNis large. As we show below, regardless ofμB, a large enoughσBwill ensure thatFOMis more robust thanCE.
To facilitate our analysis below, we establish two lemmas concerning the correlation ofCEandFOMwithS.

In contrast, we show that the efficiency loss of usingFOMrelative to the idealf∗is bounded and depends critically onw0, the fraction of unbiased forecasts. We first derive a lower bound for the case of theN=1forecast in Proposition1. We then derive a looser bound for the generalNcase in Proposition2.
Proposition 1.For theN=1forecast, the efficiency of FOM relative to the optimal but infeasible measuref∗(U)is given by


This lower bound depends only on ω0, the probability of drawing an unbiased forecast. Panel A of TableIgives the numerical value of this lower bound. In the extreme case when the variance of aggregate biases is infinitely large,FOMstill retains decent efficiency relative to the optimal but infeasible measure—the relative efficiency is 56.4% when ω0= 0.5 (the probability of unbiased forecasts is one-half), and goes up to 79.8% as ω0increases (higher probability of unbiased forecasts).
In the general case ofN>1forecasts, we can still provide a lower bound for the efficiency of our simple parameter-free measureFOMrelative to this optimal measuref∗(U), but it will not be as tight as in the case ofN=1. Our derivation assumes that, for a given set ofNforecasts, we can condition on bothUand additional information on the subset of forecasts that are unbiased. We call this subset of unbiased forecastsAand denote the size of this set by|A|, which is an integer between 0 (no forecasts are unbiased) andN(all forecasts are unbiased).
We can calculate analytically the maximal correlation of the optimalhwithSassuming that we can also condition onA. We compare the relative efficiency ofFOMto this maximal correlation and it is in this sense that this is a looser lower bound sincef∗(U)need not generate this maximal correlation.
Proposition 2.For an arbitrary number (N) of forecasts, the relative efficiency of FOM to the optimal but infeasible measuref∗(U)is given by


The numerator of the third term in11can be evaluated analytically.4Using this and the fact that|A|∼binomial(N,ω0), we can evaluate11, which depends only onN,rF, and ω0.
Panel B of TableIsummarizes the numerical values of the lower bound forN=10and various values ofw0(the fraction of unbiased forecasts) andrF(the ratio of the standard deviation of analysts' forecast errors to the standard deviation of the earnings shock). The relative efficiency increases withw0, holdingrFfixed. The relative efficiency decreases withrFholdingw0fixed. Recall that this lower bound assumes we know exactly which subset of forecasts are unbiased. But even against this unrealistic benchmark,FOMcan be relatively efficient whenw0is not too small, that is, when not too many forecasts are biased, and whenrFis low, that is, when analyst forecast errors are not too variable relative to the actual earnings uncertainty. Reasonable values forw0should be between 0.7 and 0.9, which implies that 10% to 30% of the analysts' forecasting is biased from incentives or conflicts of interest. ForrF, parameter values around 0.5 to 2 seem plausible as the standard deviation of analyst forecasts is on par with how much actual uncertainty there is about the stock. At these parameter values, the lower bound is 0.5. That is,FOM, the parameter-free measure achieves roughly 50% of the efficiency as a measure conditioning on knowing all of the parameter values as well as the set of unbiased forecasts for any given draw.
We now add tail events with small probability to the baseline model. This is motivated by the fact that, in the real data, occasionally most analysts or even all analysts miss by a huge margin. An extremeCEhas a magnitude that is multiple times greater than the majority of observations (e.g., the 3% on the two tails is 30 times the central 97% in average absolute value). In contrast, the impact of outliers onFOMis limited because, by design, the distortion is not magnified by the magnitude. Here, we formally show that the potential outliers have minimal impact on the efficiency ofFOMcompared to the baseline case.
This lower bound depends on θ (the probability of drawing outliers) and ω0(the probability of drawing an unbiased forecast in the baseline model). For reasonably small θ, the decrease in efficiency is minimal. For example, whenθ=0.05, the additional multiplier is(1−θ)1/2=0.975, so the reduction in relative efficiency is only 2.5%.
The numerical values of this lower bound forθ=0.05, which are shown in Panel C of TableI, are similar to those given in Panel A of TableIfor the baseline case.FOMstill retains decent efficiency relative to the optimal but infeasible measure—the relative efficiency is 55.0% when the probability of unbiased forecasts is one-half, and goes up to 77.8% as the probability of unbiased forecast increases.
article.II. Data:
The data on analysts' earnings forecasts are taken from the Institutional Brokers Estimate System (I/B/E/S). We gather data for U.S. stocks as well as international stocks following I/B/E/S classifications.5We conduct our analysis using the Unadjusted Detailed files. We focus on forecasts of fiscal year-end earnings (FY1) from 1983 to 2015. Stock returns, prices, and number of outstanding shares come from the Center for Research in Securities Prices (CRSP) Daily Stocks file. The forecast data are merged with actual earnings obtained from I/B/E/S and the daily stock price data from CRSP. Observations are dropped if forecast data, earnings data, or stock data are missing.
For each analyst in a given forecast period, we restrict every forecast to be made within 90 days of the annual earnings announcements. If an analyst makes more than one forecast within 90 days of the earnings announcement, we keep the latest forecast before the earnings announcements. In some records, the revision date precedes the original forecast date, which is considered an error on the part of I/B/E/S. In this case, we use the original forecast date. In addition, the FY1 earnings announcements need to fall between 15 and 90 calendar days following the fiscal period-end date; otherwise, we drop the observations.6We further remove penny stocks with a price of less than $5, we adjust the data for stock splits, and we require an earnings announcement to have at least one analyst forecast.7
Following the literature, we define the consensus error (CE) as the difference between actual FY1 earnings and the consensus forecast scaled by the stock price 20 days prior to the earnings announcement (price( − 20)). We consider both mean consensus (arithmetic mean across individual forecasts) and median consensus (50thpercentile of individual forecasts) in formulatingCE. We sortCEinto deciles and assign a rank score from 1 to 10 based on the mean consensus. As forCEbased on median consensus, which has a value of 0 for over 20% of observations, we apply a more coarse sort by rankingCEinto only six groups. For brevity, we only report results forCEusing the median consensus sinceCEusing the mean consensus performs far worse in explaining stock returns. Analyst forecast dispersion (DISP) is defined as the standard deviation of analyst forecasts scaled byprice(−20). Following the literature, we calculate earnings persistence using an AR(1) model with annual earnings. Each firm has only one persistence measure in our sample since we require all years to reliably calculate the persistence of annual earnings.
We use the functionsIActual<AllandIActual>Allto indicate when all analysts miss on the same side. The first of these functions,IActual<All, equals 1 if the minimum forecast is higher than actual earnings. In this case, all analysts are too positive and make forecasts higher than actual earnings. In contrast,IActual>Allequals 1 if all analysts are too pessimistic and the maximum forecast is lower than actual earnings. By construction,FOMequals 1 ifIActual>Allis 1 and −1 ifIActual<Allis 1. In the instances in whichN=1,FOMis 1, 0, or −1.
We concentrate on two time windows relative to earnings announcements. The first corresponds to returns cumulated over the three-day window from one trading day before until one day after the earnings release date (CAR). The second corresponds to the cumulative postannouncement returns (POSTCAR) using trading days +2 to +126 after the earnings announcement.
TableIIreports summary statistics for the variables. In Panel A, which presents results for U.S. stocks, notice that theCEusing the median consensus has a mean of −0.0043 and a standard deviation of 0.08,Rank(CE)using the median consensus has a mean of 3.5 and a standard deviation of 1.7,8andFOMhas a mean of 0.1 and a standard deviation of 0.8. In addition,IActual<Allhas a mean of 0.24 andIActual>Allhas a mean of 0.3. In Panel B, which presents these results for international stocks, we report analogous summary statistics. Overall, they are very similar to the United States, although the mean value forFOMand the mean value for misses on the same side are both lower.
In Figure3, we plot the distribution ofFOMacross the entire sample. On thex-axis are bins for various values ofFOM. Around 23% of our sample is in the −1 bin (which denotesIActual<All=1) and 30% in the 1 bin (which corresponds toIActual>All=1). For the bins in the middle, we have a bin width of 0.25. The bins with positiveFOMs each account for around 5% to 10% of the observations. The bins with negativeFOMs have a somewhat similar representation. In Figure4, we plot theFOMdistribution conditional on the number of analystsN. In Figure5, we see that the time series of misses on the same side varies over our sample period 1983 to 2015. While total misses on the same side is consistently high at 45%, all misses being above the actual have been declining, while all misses being below the actual have been increasing.
In Panel C of TableII, we report the correlation matrix for our variables of interest in whichCEis based on the median consensus. The correlation betweenCEandRank(CE)is 0.22 and the correlation betweenFOMandCEis 0.18. As we discuss above in the extension of our model with outliers, aCEthat has fat tails can drive these correlations down. The correlation ofFOMwithRank(CE)is higher at 0.84 but it is far from perfectly correlated. As a result, it will be interesting to see which of these two measures is more informative for stock returns. In any event,FOMhas different information about market surprises fromCEandRank(CE). Results in Panel D using international data are similar. One thing to note is that the outliers makeCEnot as effective a measure of market surprises asRank(CE). ButFOMdoes better than both as we show below. We winsorizeCARandPOSTCARat the 1% and 99% levels in our regression analysis below.
It is also useful to decomposeFOMinto firm and time characteristics. We consider three models: (1)FOMon firm dummies only, (2)FOMon year dummies only, and (3)FOMon firm and year dummies. TheR2for specification (1) is 0.12, that for specification (2) is 0.045, and that for specification (3) is 0.14. In other words, firm fixed effects or year dummies explain little of the variation inFOM. Rather,FOMis mostly driven by idiosyncratic events, consistent with the premise of our model.
article.III. Empirical Findings:
In TableIII, we run the canonical earnings announcement event study regression withCARas the dependent variable and various permutations ofCE,FOM,IActual<All, andIActual>Allas independent variables. All regressions include year dummies. In column (1), we see that the coefficient onCEis positive as expected but theR2is low at 0.002. In column (2),FOMattracts a coefficient of 0.0181 with at-statistic of 25.03 and anR2of 0.044. A one-standard-deviation increase inFOMincreasesCARby 1.5%, which is a sizeable three-day move in stock returns. In column (3), we find that the coefficient onIActual<Allis negative as expected with a coefficient of −0.0167 and at-statistic of −11.17. ForIActual>All, it is positive at 0.0203 with at-statistic of 13.71. Thus, the market's reaction is fairly symmetric when everyone misses on the same side, whether it is too high or too low. Again, the market reactions are sizeable—roughly a 1.7% decrease in stock prices over three days when all analysts miss too high and a 2% increase over three days when all analysts miss too low. In column (4), we find thatFOMis far more informative forCARthanCEwhen we include both variables in a multiple regression. The coefficient onCEgoes to zero while the coefficient onFOMis unchanged. It is in this sense thatFOMdominatesCE. The same holds in column (5) when we compareCEto the everyone-misses-on-the-same-side indicators.
In Panel B, we compare the relative power ofRank(CE)andFOMto explainCAR. In column (1), we find thatRank(CE)attracts a coefficient of 0.0079 with at-statistic of 20.87. TheR2is 0.037. As expected, it performs much better thanCEbecauseCEhas outliers. In column (2), the coefficient onFOMis the same as that in Panel A with anR2of 0.044, which is higher than that onRank(CE). The coefficients on the everyone-misses-on-the-same-side indicators in column (3) are the same as in Panel A. In column (4), where we combine both of these explanatory variables, we see that the coefficient onFOMis much more significant compared to that onRank(CE). Moreover, theR2is the same as whenFOMis in the regression by itself. In column (5), we find that, in a horse race betweenRank(CE)and the everyone-misses-on-the-same-side indicators,Rank(CE)retains more explanatory power. This indicates thatFOMdoes better thanRank(CE)not only when everyone misses on the same side. There is also information not captured inRank(CE)for intermediate values ofFOM.
One way to compare the economic magnitudes is to ask how a one-standard-deviation increase inRank(CE)orFOMincreases theCARfrom the point estimates in column (4). ForRank(CE), its standard deviation is 1.7, while forFOM, it is 0.8. The impliedCAReffect ofRank(CE)is just 0.004 compared to the impliedCAReffect forFOMof 0.011. TheFOMeffect is three times as large as theRank(CE)effect using the median forecast for the consensus. It is thus not surprising that theR2does not change much from the univariateFOMspecification when we addRank(CE).
In TableIV, we usePOSTCARas the dependent variable. In Panel A, we compareFOMto the unrankedCE. In column (1), we see thatCEagain attracts a positive coefficient of 0.0312 but it is not statistically significant. In column (2), the coefficient onFOMis 0.0112 with at-statistic of 3.72. In column (3), we see that the coefficients on the indicators when everyone misses on the same side are −0.0151 with at-statistic of −2.59 and 0.01 with at-statistic of 1.68. These latter two coefficients are economically interesting as we can interpret them as returns on shorting a portfolio in which everyone misses too high (negative surprise) and longing a portfolio in which everyone misses too low (positive surprise). The six-month return is 2.5%, which translates to an annualized return of 5%, which is an economically interesting magnitude. In column (4), we find thatFOMbetter explainsPOSTCAR. When we run the multiple regression, we see thatFOMis more informative aboutPOSTCARthanCE. In particular, the coefficient onCEdrops dramatically, while that onFOMis virtually unchanged. A similar conclusion holds in column (5) for the everyone-misses-on-the-same-side indicators.
In Panel B, we compare the ability ofFOMto that ofRank(CE)to explainPOSTCAR. In column (1), we see thatRank(CE)comes in significantly with a coefficient of 0.0041 and at-statistic of 2.81. Columns (2) and (3) are the same as those in Panel A. In column (4), where we include bothRank(CE)andFOM,Rank(CE)has the wrong sign, whileFOMis even more significant and in the right direction, with a coefficient of 0.0132 and at-statistic of 2.32. In column (5), where we examine how the indicators for everyone-missing-on-the-same-side do compared toRank(CE), we see thatRank(CE)is no longer significant while the coefficients on the indicators are largely unchanged.
In TableV, we rerun our analysis from Tables III and IV but for an international sample of stocks. We conduct this analysis for two reasons. First, the international sample gives us an out-of-sample test of our robustness theory. Second, our theory also predicts that the incremental improvement ofFOMoverCEshould be lower in markets where bias is potentially unimportant, that is, whereσBis not large. The literature on earnings-guidance games suggests that the issuance incentives that lead to these games and resulting bias are much less prevalent in international markets.
We break the international sample down into three groups: the United Kingdom and Canada (Panel A), Asia (Panel B), and Continental Europe (Panel C). It is easy to see that overallFOMcontinues to do better thanRank(CE), but the difference is much smaller than in the U.S. sample. This can be most easily seen for Asia, where there is little improvement inR2. In the next section, we formally use model selection criteria to show thatFOMimproves performance much more in the U.S. market, where bias is large, than in markets outside the United States.
In TableVI, we calculate BIC difference statistics (ΔBIC) to evaluate various models of earnings surprises in explainingCARandPOSTCAR. We also calculate likelihood ratio tests to compare nested model comparisons in which we evaluate a multivariate model with bothFOMandCEagainst the univariate counterparts.
The first three columns correspond to theCARregression and the second three to thePOSTCARregression. Panel A reports results for the U.S. sample. Let us first consider the BIC difference test betweenRank(CE)andFOM. The better the model fit, the more negative the BIC. The difference test is simply the difference in the BIC of the two nonnested models. A positive number means thatRank(CE)is a worse model to explainCAR. BIC has natural cutoffs to judge the severity of the lack of fit when comparing these two models. A BIC difference greater than 10 is considered strong evidence againstRank(CE). ForCAR, the BIC difference is 384. ForPOSTCAR, it is 25. We can therefore strongly chooseFOMas the superior model for predicting stock returns compared toRank(CE).
We next compareRank(CE)to a model with bothRank(CE)andFOM. Here, we can easily conclude that we would never choose a univariate model with justRank(CE). This is true both in the BIC difference test and in the likelihood ratio test with associatedp-values. The likelihood ratio test is an additional test we can run sinceRank(CE)is nested in the multiple regression model. In the third row of Panel A, we find thatFOMis inferior to a multiple regression model withFOMandRank(CE)as explanatory variables. However, forPOSTCAR, the BIC difference is −10, which means thatFOMis better than the more elaborate model.
Panels B to D report results for the three international samples. Here, we can see that the BIC difference numbers are all much smaller than for the United States, consistent withFOMnot being far superior toRank(CE)for markets outside the United States, where conflicts of interest are lower.
TableVIIis motivated by the discussion in SectionI.Don whetherFOMdoes better thanRank(CE)for long-horizon versus short-horizon forecasts. As we derive in the model, whether this is the case depends on a few parameters. One, of course, is the extent of the bias. We know that the mean bias is more positive for long-horizon forecasts from the walk-down of earnings forecasts literature (i.e.,μBmore positive). But, at the same time, it is well known that long-horizon forecasts are noisier than short-horizon forecasts (i.e.,σFis higher). And there are potentially large biases for near-term forecasts (i.e.,σBmight be large). As such, it is not clear how theFOMcorrection should perform when we consider forecasts issued further back in time.
In TableVII, Panel A (B) reports the results where we require the forecasts to be issued at least three (six) months before the announcement date. We find thatFOMis still better thanRank(CE)for these forecasts issued further out. But the incremental performance improvement is less pronounced than for the most recent forecasts featured in our baseline tests in TableIII. To the extent that one is interested in explaining stock returns, the most recent forecasts would be the most relevant ones to consider, and hereFOMis a much better model as we have established.
In TableVIII, we test the robustness of our results above by conducting subsample analysis. In Panel A, we split the sample by the median earnings persistence of a stock. Annual earnings persistence is described by an AR(1) coefficient of 0.38 on average. The improvement inR2from usingFOMrather thanRank(CE)is similar across the two subsamples. In Panel B, we split the sample by the median dispersion in analyst forecasts (DISP) and find similar results across the two subsamples. Overall, these results further highlight the importance of usingFOMfor U.S. stocks.
In our analysis so far, we focus on forecasts for year-end earnings that are within 90 days before the announcement date. We obtain similar results when we drop such screens, when we use quarterly instead of annual year-end earnings forecasts, and when the benchmark excess returns used are the Daniel et al. (1997) returns that account for size, book-to-market, and momentum.
In TableIX, we compare the relative performance ofCEorRank(CE)andFOMin explaining revisions in analyst expectations (between two adjacent fiscal years) in the same direction as market returns. If bothRank(CE)andFOMare picking up surprises, then positive surprises should be followed by positive revisions of the consensus forecast. However, when it comes to comparing which is more powerful, any conclusion becomes more involved since we know from our analysis that a subset of analyst forecasts can be biased and that these biased forecasts influence the consensus. So, the answer also depends on how the biased analysts revise their expectations, which is difficult to say. In any event, since part of the consensus is unbiased and similar to the market, we expectFOMto still have power to predict revisions of the consensus. This is indeed what we find. When we look the economic significance of the coefficients onCEorRank(CE)andFOMand examine what a one-standard-deviation shock to these variables implies for the consensus revision, we still find thatFOMis stronger thanCEorRank(CE)in Panels A and B. In sum, it is comforting thatFOMand the everyone-misses-on-the-same-side indicators are picking up revisions of the consensus.
We repeat the same regressions as in SectionsIII.AandIII.Bby replacingCEandRank(CE)with optimal-weightedCEandRank(CE). The results are reported in TableXfor earnings announcement returns. The parameter-dependentCEis not much better than simply usingCE. In Panel B, we use the parameter-dependentRank(CE)instead ofRank(CE)and obtain similar conclusions as before. The reason of course is that the bias and precision of the individual forecasts are estimated with error and this can negate the advantage of trying to optimally adjust for these parameters in calculating the consensus forecast. In sum, the difficulty of estimating bias and precision highlights the value of using a parameter-free method.
article.IV. Conclusion:
An important part of event studies of earnings announcements is capturing whether the market is surprised. The traditional measure used is the difference between realized earnings and the consensus forecast, defined as the average or median of individual forecasts. We argue, however, that the fraction of forecasts that miss on the same side does a better job explaining stock returns than the consensus error because individual forecasts can be biased. The ideal but difficult to estimate parameter-dependent measure of market surprise is a nonlinear filter of individual errors that adjusts for individual forecast bias. We show thatCEis a poor parameter-free approximation of this ideal measure, while the fraction of misses on the same side (FOM), which discards the magnitude of misses, offers a far better approximation. We demonstrate the empirical validity of our theoretical analysis using earnings forecast data from a global sample of markets. While our paper focuses on earnings forecasts, the methodology that we lay out can be applied equally well to any type of forecast such as those on macrovariables. We believe that the methodology can be used to improve the precision of event studies of capital market efficiency, which are a basic tool for economists.
Editors: Bruno Biais, Michael R. Roberts, and Kenneth J. Singleton
